note_data.title: Atoms, molecules and stoichiometry
keyPoints: **Relative atomic mass** is the weighted mean mass of an atom of an element compared to 1/12 of the mass of an atom of carbon-12
**Relative molecular mass** is the sum of the relative atomic masses of all atoms in a molecule
**One mole** is the amount of substance that contains as many elementary entities as there are atoms in exactly 12g of carbon-12
**Avogadro constant** (L) = 6.02 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, representing the number of particles in one mole
**Empirical formula** shows the simplest whole number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound
**Molecular formula** shows the actual number of atoms of each element in one molecule of a compound
**Stoichiometric calculations** use molar ratios from balanced equations to calculate quantities of reactants and products
**Limiting reagent** is the reactant that is completely consumed first and determines the amount of product formed

definitions.definition: The amount of substance that contains as many elementary entities as there are atoms in exactly 12g of carbon-12
The simplest whole number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound
The weighted mean mass of an atom of an element compared to 1/12 of the mass of an atom of carbon-12
The number of particles in one mole of any substance, equal to 6.02 × 10²³ mol⁻¹

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the formula or fact in one sentence with no explanation
Show working clearly with correct units and appropriate significant figures
Use calculations or reasoning to find the answer, showing method clearly
Give precise definition using mark scheme language exactly

modelAnswer.question: A compound contains 40.0% carbon, 6.7% hydrogen and 53.3% oxygen by mass. Calculate the empirical formula of this compound. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: C: 40.0 ÷ 12.0 = 3.33 mol, H: 6.7 ÷ 1.0 = 6.7 mol, O: 53.3 ÷ 16.0 = 3.33 mol. Divide by smallest (3.33): C = 1, H = 2.01 ≈ 2, O = 1. Empirical formula = CH₂O
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct calculation of moles for each element
Mark 2: Dividing by smallest number of moles

commonMistakes: Never use Mr instead of Ar for relative atomic mass - they are different quantities
Students often forget to balance equations before doing stoichiometric calculations
Never round intermediate calculations - only round the final answer to appropriate significant figures
Students confuse empirical formula with molecular formula - empirical is the simplest ratio
Never write percentage yield greater than 100% without explaining experimental errors
examinerTips: Stoichiometry questions appear in every Paper 1 and Paper 2 - master the calculations
Always show working for calculations even if using a calculator
Check your empirical formula makes chemical sense - watch for common ratios like 1:2:1
In limiting reagent questions, calculate products from each reactant separately to find which gives less product
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Mass spectrometer diagram showing ionization, acceleration, deflection and detection stages for determining relative atomic masses
